subject: Urology
title: Varicocele: Why It's Almost Always Left
hook: A bag of worms that empties when he lies down, and almost always on the left. Here is why.

points.heading: The classic vignette
points.body: A varicocele is dilated pampiniform plexus veins felt as a bag of worms. It empties on lying down and reappears on standing. One that fails to empty, or an isolated right-sided varicocele, is a red flag for a retroperitoneal mass.

points.heading: Why the left side dominates
points.body: The left testicular vein is longer and joins the left renal vein at a right angle, raising pressure. The left renal vein can also be compressed between the aorta and SMA, the nutcracker effect.

points.heading: How it is diagnosed
points.body: Diagnose on ultrasound when the vein diameter exceeds about 3 mm with venous reflux on Valsalva. Diameter alone is not enough; you need the reflux. Confirm with colour Doppler standing or on Valsalva.

points.heading: Fertility and management
points.body: Raised scrotal temperature impairs spermatogenesis and can cause oligospermia. Varicocele is found in 15 to 20% of men but around 40% of infertile men. Consider repair for testicular atrophy, abnormal semen analysis or significant symptoms; otherwise reassure.

caption: Varicocele is a guaranteed mark if you can explain why it is almost always left: a longer vein, a right-angle renal entry, and the nutcracker compression between aorta and SMA. Add the bag of worms that empties on lying down and the reflux on Valsalva, and the vignette writes itself. Remember the red flag: an isolated right-sided or non-emptying varicocele needs investigation for a retroperitoneal cause. Recall Engine turns any lecture into SBAs, flashcards and high-yield notes.
